title: Raiyar-Ich Is Writing a Different Story for Kashmir
content:
By Sahil Manzoor Bhatti
The first light touches Raiyar-Ich and the village stirs with birdsong.
Lush forests wrap the slopes, while the Sukhnag River murmurs through the valley like a gentle melody. Stars crowd the clear night sky, and bees hum among vibrant blooms.
This corner of Tehsil Khansahib in District Budgam feels alive with colour and calm. Travellers who arrive here discover more than scenery. They meet a community rooted in generations of harmony.
Raiyar-Ich sits just 12 kilometers from Srinagar’s bustling Lal Chowk, but it belongs to a different pace.
The village forms a Panchayat Halqa with nine wards, including Bhatti Mohallah, Dinda Mohallah, Tantray Mohallah, Sheikh Mohallah, and Khan Mohallah. Locals trace the name to Kashmiri words: “Rai” for lost and “Yaar” for friend.
Sufi saint Sheikh Noor-ud-Din Wali sent his companions outward from these forests to share teachings, then walked alone toward the Dreygam cave.
That spirit of connection and journey still guides the place. Residents welcome visitors with genuine warmth and live by traditions that bind them together.
Nearby Doodhpathri draws global attention with its meadows and streams. Raiyar-Ich gains from that fame while adding its own draws: Mujpathri, Sotchal Pathri, Arizal, and hidden spots that invite exploration.
The cool evening breeze carries freshness, while traditional maize bread, fragrant rajma, and rich walnuts mark local tables. Families raise livestock on time-honoured grazing lands and tend orchards that have sustained them for centuries.
Tourism now supplements these roots. Hotels, guides, and small businesses employ young people and bring steady income to households. The shift feels organic rather than imposed.
Budgam itself holds deep layers. Once larger, the district contributed land to Srinagar. Its spiritual heart beats at Charar-e-Shareef, the shrine of Sheikh-ul-Alam. Tosamaidan, Sukhnag, and other landscapes wait for more visitors. Raiyar-Ich sits at the center of this web, a peaceful hub for surrounding areas. Its people see progress within reach. They built on natural gifts and now seek tools to secure the next chapter.
Education stands high among those needs. The existing high school serves many villages, but students travel daily to Khansahib for higher studies. Families shoulder those costs with effort. Upgrading the school to higher secondary level would keep learning close and open doors wider.
Livestock remains vital for the village. Protecting grazing areas preserves a way of life that feeds families and maintains the landscape.
Skill centers focused on computers and practical trades would equip unemployed youth for broader careers. Housing schemes that reach deserving homes would improve daily comfort and signal real commitment to rural futures. Road work on the Bhatti Mohallah-Tangnar link would ease movement, connect tourists to authentic experiences, and spark new economic flows.
Each request grows from lived reality, as locals know their

title: Gadkari Announces Rs 1,500 Cr Four-Laning of Srinagar-Sonamarg Road
content:
Drass- Union Minister for Road Transport and Highways Nitin Gadkari on Tuesday described the breakthrough achieved in the Zojila Tunnel project as a “golden day” in India’s infrastructure history, saying the strategic project would emerge as a lifeline for the people of Jammu and Kashmir and Ladakh.
The milestone was achieved after engineers blasted through the final stretch of rock inside the tunnel, connecting excavation works from both ends of the 13-km tunnel being built beneath the Zojila Pass.
“Today is a golden day in India’s infrastructure history. I am happy that this state-of-the-art tunnel will become a lifeline for the people of Jammu and Kashmir and Ladakh,” Gadkari said after performing the breakthrough blast.
The Union minister said the project, being constructed in extremely challenging weather and terrain conditions, would ensure all-weather connectivity between Kashmir and Ladakh, ending the seasonal isolation caused by heavy snowfall at the Zojila Pass.
Calling it a gift to the people of Jammu and Kashmir and Ladakh, Gadkari said the approach works for the tunnel are expected to be completed by February 2028.
Congratulating engineers, workers and officials associated with the project, he said the achievement was the result of years of work carried out in harsh weather conditions.
“The workers and engineers have worked in extreme cold and difficult conditions. This tunnel has all the modern safety features and has also generated employment opportunities for local people,” he said.
Gadkari said the government had also managed to substantially reduce the cost of the project.
“The estimated cost had reached around Rs 12,000 crore. However, we are completing it at nearly Rs 7,000 crore, resulting in savings of about Rs 5,000 crore,” he said.
The minister expressed gratitude to labourers, consultants, engineers, officials of Megha Engineering and Infrastructure Limited (MEIL), which is executing the project, and the National Highways and Infrastructure Development Corporation Limited (NHIDCL).
Announcing a series of infrastructure initiatives for Jammu and Kashmir and Ladakh, Gadkari said significant progress was also being made on the Jammu-Srinagar National Highway.
“Twenty tunnels on the highway have already been completed, while work is underway on five more. Once these are completed, travel time between Jammu and Srinagar will be reduced to around three hours. It is my dream and I assure you that it will be realised soon,” he said.
Jammu and Kashmir Lieutenant Governor Manoj Sinha described the breakthrough as a historic moment and congratulated the project team for achieving the milestone despite difficult working conditions.
“The pace of development in Jammu and Kashmir and Ladakh will boost tourism, strengthen connectivity and improve national security,” Sinha said.
Expressing confidence that the remaining work would be completed on schedule, he said the Zojila Tunnel, together with the recently completed Sonamarg Tunnel, wou

title: Jammu IGP Urges Officers to Stay Operationally Ready for Amarnath Yatra
content:
Jammu- The Jammu IGP has ordered all officers to be operationally ready to ensure a safe and successful Amarnath Yatra commencing on July 3, an official said on Tuesday.
Jammu Zone Inspector General of Police (IGP) Bhim Sen Tuti made the direction while chairing a meeting here to assess security ahead of the 57-day annual pilgrimage, a police spokesperson said.
Officers from the Army, CRPF, BSF, SSB, CISF, ITBP, Intelligence agencies, Traffic Police, SDRF and Railways Police attended the meeting.
Addressing the meeting, Tuti emphasised the need for enhanced vigilance and seamless inter-agency coordination.
He also stressed the importance of proper briefing of incoming central Paramilitary forces (CPMF).
Security measures at the Bhagawati Nagar reception centre, base camps, and lodgement centres along the Yatra route were reviewed in the meeting.
The discussions focused on deployment plans, intelligence coordination, surveillance, access control measures, convoy security, traffic management and emergency response preparedness to ensure safe and smooth conduct of the pilgrimage, the spokesperson said.
